data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2021 ET - Japanese stocks are higher as some bargain hunting kicks in following their recent declines despite continuing uncertainty over U.S. tariffs. Electronics and heavy industry stocks are leading gains. TDK is up 3.0% and Lasertec is 1.5% higher while Kawasaki Heavy Industries is up 2.4% and IHI is 1.4% higher. USD/JPY is at 142.28, compared with 142.36 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any tariff-related news ahead of the earnings season set to start next week. The Nikkei Stock Average is up 0.2% at 33986.96. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

1942 ET - Japanese stocks may decline as uncertainty persists over U.S. tariffs and their economic implications. Nikkei futures are down 0.4% at 33820 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 141.69, down from 142.36 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any tariff-related news ahead of the earnings season set to start next week. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 1.0% to 33920.40 on Wednesday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1639 ET - In a speech and Q&A session in Chicago, Fed Chair Jerome Powell emphasized that keeping long-run inflation expectations in check has to be one of the central bank's overarching priorities. His comments suggest a fairly high bar for further monetary easing, analysts at LHMeyer write, indicating that Powell wants to "verify, not just trust, that high inflation won't persist." Policymakers want to be "certain that a one-time increase in the price level does not become an ongoing inflation problem," Powell said in his speech. In LHMeyer's view, his comments mean that "balancing the employment and inflation objectives in the current environment will likely involve tolerating more real-side weakness than otherwise." (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1551 ET - Treasury Yields fall for the third consecutive day as Powell says tariffs could make it harder to decide on interest rates. "Fed Chair Powell suggested that patience was the best course," as opposed to cutting rates fast, Global X's Scott Helfstein writes. March retail sales were better than expected, while industrial production fell more than forecast. Weekly jobless claims tomorrow are expected to increase slightly, in a WSJ survey. Bond markets are set to close at 2 p.m. ET tomorrow and the whole day on Good Friday. The 10-year yield declines 0.046 percentage point to 4.276% and the two-year falls 0.043 p.p. to 3.784%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1500 ET - Fitch downgrades certain Los Angeles Department of Water and Power debt, including $5.3 billion of water system revenue bonds, to AA- from AA, citing risks related to wildfire liability. "California's unique and rigid interpretation of inverse condemnation has been cited in litigation against the water system's operations," Fitch says. The firm notes that the empty Santa Ynez reservoir has been mentioned in litigation as a factor in delayed containment. "Precedent around similar litigation against water utilities is sparse and creates uncertainty around any potential liability LADWP may incur. However, Fitch believes that the prospective liability, together with the potential for increased operating or capital costs, could more readily be absorbed at the 'AA-' rating level." (josh.beckerman@wsj.com)

1443 ET - Treasury yields decline as Fed Chair Powell says at a conference in Chicago that tariffs could simultaneously raise prices and unemployment, making decisions on interest rates more difficult. He indicates the Fed would wait for more clarity before resuming interest-rate cutting. Odds of a hold in May are at 85%, up from 80% a week ago, CME data show. The 10-year yield is at 4.285% and the two-year at 3.794%, both erasing gains recorded over the past week. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1425 ET - The Bank of Canada is likely to keep its policy interest rate unchanged for an extended period, says Bank of Nova Scotia economist Derek Holt. He argues that after Canada's April 28 election, fiscal support for the economy is likely to intensify. The two main parties, Liberals and Conservatives, are promising tax relief and financial support for trade-exposed businesses and its workers. Fiscal policy will do some of the BOC's work in terms of providing economic support, Holt says. In a trade-conflict context, it's crucial the BOC be wary of cutting too deeply, he says, arguing this could trigger a new round of inflationary pressure akin to what transpired after the Covid-19 pandemic. (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1419 ET - The Bank of Canada decision to pause risks larger interest rate cuts later, Citi's Veronica Clark suggests. The economist says risks seem to be more clearly shifting to weaker inflation outcomes, and both of the scenarios presented by the central bank indicate Canadian growth slows and inflation is below 2% this year. With the current tariff backdrop likely to persist for at least three to six months and upcoming data set to weaken more clearly, Citi now expects a full percentage point of rate of cuts this year: a quarter point in June, half point in July and another quarter point in September. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1411 ET - Treasury yields decline as Fed Chair Powell speaks at a conference in Chicago. The monetary policymaker says tariffs could raise prices and unemployment at the same time, making decisions on interest rates harder to make. He indicates the Fed would wait for more clarity before resuming interest rate cutting. Odds of a hold in May are at 85%, up from 80% a week ago, CME data show. The 10-year yield is at 4.285% and the two-year at 3.794%, both erasing gains recorded over the past week. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1339 ET - The strength in March U.S. retail sales isn't only about consumers trying to get ahead of tariffs, Wells Fargo's Tim Quinlan and Shannon Grein write. Sales beat WSJ consensus in both the headline and the ex-auto figures. The report included upward revisions of previous data. Autos saw the biggest March gain, likely reflecting a rush to buy vehicles before any price increase due to tariffs. But consumers "also spent more at restaurants in March, and we're not aware of a way you can front-run tariffs with a nice night out," Quinlan and Grein say. "How consumers act in these next couple of months will be telling," they say. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1310 ET - The government of Canada's most populous province is moving to drop barriers to trade and labor mobility in the country with the introduction of a "Protect Ontario Through Free Trade Within Canada Act." Premier Doug Ford says the economy has long been held back by red tape and regulations, making Canada vulnerable to external threats, including from President Trump's tariffs. The move is part of a wave of support federally and provincially for easing trade within Canada to help counter the hit from Trump's trade policies. Ontario's act wold allow workers from other parts of the country to more easily work in the province, allow more regulated health professions to begin practicing in Ontario while waiting for registration, allow direct-to-consumer alcohol sales with reciprocating provinces, and remove all party-specific exceptions under the Canadian Free Trade Agreement. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1306 ET - Fitch Ratings says it lowered its forecasts for 2025 world growth by 0.4 percentage points, given the escalation in the global trade war. "World growth is projected to fall below 2% this year, which would be the weakest since 2009 excluding the pandemic," according to Fitch. Growth in the U.S. and in China has been cut by 0.5 pp, according to Fitch. Annual growth in the U.S. is expected at 1.2%, but will slow through 2025 to register just 0.4% year-over-year in 4Q25, Fitch says. In China, growth is expected to fall below 4% this year and in 2026. In the euro zone, growth expectations are stuck below 1%, Fitch adds. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

data.title: Alcoa Returns to Q1 Profit as Revenue Rises; Reaffirms Guidance
data.description:
Alcoa reported first-quarter adjusted income of $2.15 per share, compared with a loss of $0.81 a year earlier.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected $1.68.

The company reported a revenue of $3.37 billion, up from $2.6 billion a year earlier. Analysts polled by FactSet expected $3.47 billion.

The company expects 2025 total alumina segment production and shipments to remain unchanged from its prior projection, ranging between 9.5 million to 9.7 million metric tons and between 13.1 million and 13.3 million metric tons, respectively.

The company reaffirmed its 2025 total aluminum segment production guidance and shipments, ranging between 2.3 million and 2.5 million metric tons and between 2.6 million and 2.8 million metric tons, respectively.

The company's shares were up almost 1% in recent Thursday trade.

data.title: Select Harvests Target Price Raised 3.4% to A$6.00/Share by Barrenjoey>SHV.AU
Navigator Global Target Price Cut 3.2% to A$3.00/Share by Barrenjoey>NGI.AU
Iluka Target Price Cut 1.8% to A$5.35/Share by Barrenjoey>ILU.AU
Baudroie Fiscal Year Attributable Profit Jumps 55%
data.description:
Baudroie reported a 55% increase in attributable profit to 1.80 billion yen for the year ended February, according to a Wednesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

Earnings per share rose to 112.55 yen from 72.7 yen a year earlier. Revenue climbed 59% to 11.65 billion yen.

The technology firm forecasts attributable profit to grow 30% to 2.35 billion yen in the current fiscal year. EPS is projected at 151.57 yen, while revenue is expected to rise 33% to 15.5 billion yen.

Baudroie did not pay a dividend for the fiscal year just ended but plans a year-end dividend of 15.16 yen for the current year.

data.title: Rio Tinto Target Price Cut 1.6% to A$125.00/Share by Barrenjoey>RIO.AU
Under Trump, consumer watchdog to slash industry supervision
data.description:
The top U.S. watchdog agency for consumer finance will cut its inspections of financial services companies in half while focusing on threats to military personnel and turning away from areas like student loans, medical debt, digital payments and consumer data, according to a memo sent to staff on Wednesday.

The decision indicates the U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau intends to continue supervision and enforcement activities even though President Donald Trump and billionaire Elon Musk, whom Trump charged with overseeing government cost-cutting efforts, earlier this year called for the agency's elimination.

The changes, however, point to a fundamental reorientation of priorities away from areas that had been core to the CFPB's work in previous administrations.

CFPB representatives did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

"The bureau will focus its enforcement and supervision resources on pressing threats to consumers, particularly service members and their families and veterans," Mark Paoletta, the chief legal officer, wrote, according to a copy of the memo seen by Reuters.

The memo, which was first shared on X by a Wall Street Journal reporter, said all prior CFPB documents setting enforcement and supervision priorities had been rescinded.

Created in 2010 following the 2008 financial crisis, the CFPB is the sole federal agency with power to enforce consumer financial laws at nonbank institutions such as mortgage originators and payment services. But the memo said the CFPB would reverse recent trends and focus more on "the largest banks" rather than nonbanks, which it said now got most of the bureau's attention.

The agency's work involves direct supervision of such companies as well as enforcement through the courts. However, after Trump fired the CFPB's director in February, the White House initially froze all enforcement and supervisory activities and dismissed pending cases en masse.

Paoletta said the agency would focus less on matters where state-level agencies also have jurisdiction and would cut the number of supervisory "events" by 50%, sharply reducing the supervision of nonbanks.

The bureau will also "deprioritize" its oversight of financial services for prisoners and detainees, peer-to-peer platforms, digital payments and consumer data, Paoletta said.

Critics have assailed Musk's involvement at the CFPB through the so-called Department of Government Efficiency as a conflict of interest due to plans by his social media company X to offer payment services. Musk and the White House have rejected such criticism.

data.title: Aurelia Metals Target Price Raised 15% to A$0.30/Share by Jefferies>AMI.AU
Mount Gibson's Non-Executive Chairman Steps Down
data.description:
Mount Gibson Iron said Non Executive Chairman Lee Seng Hui resigned, according to a Wednesday filing with the Australian bourse.

Brett Smith, executive director of Metals X , will succeed Lee, the filing said.

data.title: Kanoria Chemicals Forms Two Subsidiaries Overseas
data.description:
Kanoria Chemicals & Industries has incorporated two step-down subsidiaries overseas for the development and production of electronic devices in the automotive sector, according to a Wednesday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

CoSyst Control Systems GmbH, a step-down subsidiary of the company, has incorporated two wholly-owned subsidiaries—Adapt Control Systems in Germany and Adapt Control Systems in Canada.

data.title: Euronet Announces First Quarter 2025 Earnings Release Date and Conference Call Details
data.description:
LEAWOOD, Kan., April 16, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Euronet (or the “Company”) announced today it will release first quarter 2025 earnings results prior to the market opening on Thursday, April 24, 2025. Euronet will hold a conference call the same day at 9:00 a.m. Eastern Time to discuss the results.

The conference call and accompanying slide show presentation will be accessible via webcast by following the link posted on http://ir.euronetworldwide.com. Participants wanting to access the conference call by telephone must register at   Euronet Worldwide First Quarter 2025 Earnings Call to receive dial-in information. While not required, it is recommended participants join the call five minutes prior to the event start.

A webcast replay will be available beginning approximately one hour after the event at http://ir.euronetworldwide.com and will remain available for one year.

About Euronet Worldwide, Inc.

Starting in Central Europe in 1994 and growing to a global real-time digital and cash payments network with millions of touchpoints today, Euronet now moves money in all the ways consumers and businesses depend upon. This includes money transfers, credit/debit card processing, ATMs, POS services, branded payments, foreign currency exchange and more. With products and services in more than 200 countries and territories provided through its own brand and branded business segments, Euronet and its financial technologies and networks make participation in the global economy easier, faster, and more secure for everyone. 

A leading global financial technology solutions and payments provider, Euronet has developed an extensive global payments network that includes 55,248 installed ATMs, approximately 1,160,000 EFT POS terminals and a growing portfolio of outsourced debit and credit card services which are under management in 64 countries; card software solutions; a prepaid processing network of approximately 777,000 POS terminals at approximately 362,000 retailer locations in 64 countries; and a global money transfer network of approximately 607,000 locations serving 197 countries and territories. Euronet serves clients from its corporate headquarters in Leawood, Kansas, USA, and 67 worldwide offices. For more information, please visit the Company's website at www.euronetworldwide.com.

Contact:

Euronet Worldwide, Inc.

Stephanie Taylor

T: 913-327-4200

E: staylor@eeft.com

data.title: BHP Bull Cheers Solution to Escondida Output DipMarket Talk
data.description: BHP reported robust 3Q output and, importantly, has been able to find a way to avoid the sharp reduction in Escondida output beyond FY27, says Citi analyst Paul McTaggart. "We don't have details but it includes extending the life of the oldest concentrator beyond FY29," he says in a note. That's "a good outcome for Escondida following the disappointments of the November site visit last year," McTaggart adds. Citi has a buy rating and A$45.00 target on BHP, which is up 0.5% at A$36.24. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: Japan Export Growth Slows
data.description:
Japan's exports rose 3.9% yoy in March 2025, falling short of market expectations for a 4.5% increase and easing sharply from an 11.4% surge in the prior month.

Still, it was the sixth consecutive month of growth, with the value reaching a three-month high of JPY 9,847.8 billion.

Japan Imports Rise Less than Expected
data.description: Japan's imports grew by 2.0% yoy to JPY 9,303.8 billion in March 2025, rebounding from a 0.7% decline in February but falling short of the projected 3.1% rise.

South Korea's Kospi 0.3% Higher; Defense, Shipbuilding Stocks AdvanceMarket Talk
data.description: South Korea's benchmark Kospi rises 0.3% to 2454.81 in early trade, as defense and shipbuilding stocks advance. The market impact from new U.S. trade restrictions on Nvidia's chips is easing as local stocks priced in much of it a day earlier, Kiwoom Securities says in a note. Trading volume is thin ahead of the Bank of Korea's rate decision later in the day. Fighter-jet maker Korea Aerospace Industries rises 2.6%. Shipbuilder HD Hyundai Heavy Industries gains 2.8%. USD/KRW is 0.5% lower at 1,419.25 in Seoul onshore trading. South Korea's 10-year government bond yield is down 3.5 bps at 2.629%. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.title: James Hardie Target Price Cut 13% to A$41.00/Share by Barrenjoey>JHX.AU
Is Harvard's $53 Billion Endowment Big Enough to Offset Federal Funding Cuts?
data.description:
By Gretchen Tarrant Gulla, Brian McGill and Xavier Martinez

Harvard told Trump no. The question is, can it afford to?

Shortly after the university formally refused the administration's demands for broad oversight of its admissions, hiring and culture over campus antisemitism concerns, the government announced a $2.26 billion freeze of Harvard's multiyear grants and contracts.

Despite its significant resources, the university is limited in how it can spend endowment money and, thus, is less insulated from federal funding cuts than it may appear. Harvard, in its financial report, called the idea that endowments can be "accessed like checking accounts" a "common misconception."

Here's how the finances of the wealthiest university in America break down:

Harvard's endowment totals $53.2 billion, the largest of any U.S. university. Yet more than 80% is restricted to specific use cases, limiting how the university can choose to spend its money. Among individual schools with endowments of $20 billion or more, Harvard has the second-highest proportion of restricted funds.

Most universities make about 5% of their endowment's market value available for operations annually. That disbursement includes a mix of both restricted and unrestricted funds. In Harvard's case, the annual distribution is applied uniformly across all endowment funds. Last fiscal year, Harvard distributed 5% of its endowment for operations.

That endowment distribution of $2.4 billion was the largest source of operations funding for Harvard during fiscal year 2024, making up 37% of the $6.5 billion pool. Federal funding made up roughly 11% of that total, or $686 million.

Because the $2.26 billion federal funding freeze includes grants and contracts that span several years, it is unclear how cuts will affect Harvard's budget in the near term. Federal funding rose 1% year over year between fiscal years 2023 and 2024. Assuming the same trend, that could leave the university with up to a $693 million gap in federal funding this fiscal year.

Harvard has several levers at its disposal to try to bridge that gap.

One might be increasing its annual endowment distribution, typically between 5% and 5.5% of the total market value.

However, one-fifth of Harvard's $43 billion in restricted funds is what the school considers "perpetual endowment" — money that is not intended to be removed under any circumstances. Additionally, Massachusetts law limits endowment spending, meaning the state could get involved if it believes distributions exceed what is "prudent for the uses, benefits, purposes and duration for which the endowment fund is established."

And while Harvard's annual distribution is, in some cases, less restrictive than the endowment principal, only 30% of those funds were available for general mission support in fiscal year 2024.

The university is limited in its ability to reallocate most of its endowment, but it can borrow against it. In a sign that Harvard anticipated a potential cash crunch, the university issued $750 million in taxable debt earlier this month. That would roughly cover the maximum amount of annual funding at risk.

Alternatively, the university could look to reduce its expenses to offset the funding loss. Harvard's expenses grew 9% last year, driven primarily by personnel costs.

Write to Gretchen Tarrant Gulla at gretchen.tarrant@wsj.com, Brian McGill at brian.mcgill@wsj.com and Xavier Martinez at xavier.martinez@wsj.com

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2020 ET - South Korea's benchmark Kospi rises 0.3% to 2454.81 in early trade, as defense and shipbuilding stocks advance. The market impact from new U.S. trade restrictions on Nvidia's chips is easing as local stocks priced in much of it a day earlier, Kiwoom Securities says in a note. Trading volume is thin ahead of the Bank of Korea's rate decision later in the day. Fighter-jet maker Korea Aerospace Industries rises 2.6%. Shipbuilder HD Hyundai Heavy Industries gains 2.8%. USD/KRW is 0.5% lower at 1,419.25 in Seoul onshore trading. South Korea's 10-year government bond yield is down 3.5 bps at 2.629%. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

1942 ET - Japanese stocks may decline as uncertainty persists over U.S. tariffs and their economic implications. Nikkei futures are down 0.4% at 33820 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 141.69, down from 142.36 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any tariff-related news ahead of the earnings season set to start next week. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 1.0% to 33920.40 on Wednesday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1934 ET - Australia's S&P/ASX 200 looks set for an opening fall after U.S. equities retreated and Fed Chair Jerome Powell warned that the central bank has limited flexibility to mitigate the impact of the Trump administration's trade war. ASX futures are down 0.3% ahead of Thursday's session, the last of the week ahead of a public holiday on Friday. Ahead of the open, BHP reported a 10% rise in 3Q copper output and flat iron-ore production. Woodside Energy announced an LNG sale and purchase agreement with Uniper. Falls in shares of chip companies sent U.S. indices lower. The S&P 500 lost 2.2%, the DJIA shed 1.7%, and the Nasdaq Composite fell 3.1%. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1828 ET - Kinder Morgan discloses how countries are responding to Trump's tariffs threats when it comes to U.S. natural gas exports. South Korea and Indonesia are already in specific discussions on increasing U.S. liquefied natural gas exports to improve their negotiation position regarding U.S. tariffs, CEO Kimberly Allen Dang says on a call with analysts. The European Union will also increase its use of U.S. LNG as it seems to be reluctant to ever again allow Russia to be the dominant supplier of natural gas to Europe, Dang says. In addition to growth in LNG feed gas demand, the company sees "nice upticks" in exports to Mexico, with power demand driven in part by the surge in AI and data centers and residential commercial use, the executive adds. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1815 ET - The uncertainty of tariffs, along with commodity prices, made Kinder Morgan be a little bit more conservative in communicating its outlook for the year, CEO Kimberly Allen Dang says on a call with analysts. However, the energy-infrastructure company is seeing growing natural gas demand in the U.S. It also expects any loss of the Chinese market to be more than offset by the efforts of governments in the European Union and Asia to increase the imports of U.S. liquefied natural gas to reduce trade imbalances and put themselves in a better negotiation position with regard to U.S. tariffs, Dang says. "China has not imported any U.S. liquefied natural gas since February, and yet peak gas demand is setting records," she adds. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1809 ET - Five Below shares appear to have reached a bottom after a slew of product, management and shrink problems last year and current tariff worries, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. They say the stock's valuation is at all-time lows, trading as if the company were a quarter of its size a decade ago as investors price in a worst-case scenario. But even as margins take a hit from tariffs, the company is likely to see improving revenue trends, unit base and shrink, and the allure of the store model and business position should only strengthen, the analysts say. Those tailwinds and further clarity on tariffs should help shares get a boost from their currently very cheap levels, they add. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1749 ET [Dow Jones]--Lyft's acquisition of taxi-hailing app Freenow adds a notable new competitor for Uber in Europe, D.A. Davidson analysts say in a research note. The analysts say it's unclear how Freenow's revenue breaks down by country, but 75% of the cities it currently operates in are located in Germany and Poland, according to the company's website. The most important dynamic for Uber investors, though, is whether Lyft can help Freenow gain share in Uber's key market in the UK, the analysts say. They say Freenow appears to currently operate in 10 different cities in the UK. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1717 ET - Natural gas demand keeps growing in the U.S., Kinder Morgan says after reporting higher revenue in the 4Q. "The landscape for natural gas continues to be more and more favorable," the company says, adding that for the second year in a row, the U.S. set a first quarter record for demand, which grew by 6.8 billion cubic feet per day compared to the same period a year earlier. "Residential/commercial natural demand and liquefied natural gas feedgas demand were up 10% and 15%, respectively, in the latest quarter," the company says. Shares rise 0.4% to $27.05 in post-market trading. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1645 ET - Lean hog futures closed trading up 2.9%, to 97.925 cents a pound, with some traders holding short positions being "flushed out" ahead of the holiday-shortened week, says Karl Setzer of Consus Ag Consulting. "The U.S. pork inventory has been dropping and this is keeping the contract supported as well," said Setzer. Live cattle closed up 1.1%, to $2.02075 a pound. Setzer says traders are preparing for tomorrow's USDA Cattle on Feed report, which is expected to show a decline in the total inventory of cattle on U.S. feedlots. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1639 ET - In a speech and Q&A session in Chicago, Fed Chair Jerome Powell emphasized that keeping long-run inflation expectations in check has to be one of the central bank's overarching priorities. His comments suggest a fairly high bar for further monetary easing, analysts at LHMeyer write, indicating that Powell wants to "verify, not just trust, that high inflation won't persist." Policymakers want to be "certain that a one-time increase in the price level does not become an ongoing inflation problem," Powell said in his speech. In LHMeyer's view, his comments mean that "balancing the employment and inflation objectives in the current environment will likely involve tolerating more real-side weakness than otherwise." (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1611 ET - Fed Chair Powell's words of caution weigh on already weak U.S. stock prices. He indicates that tariffs could make it harder to decide on interest rate cuts. The technology sector drops 4% and is the biggest loser in the S&P 500. Nvidia falls 7% after it took a $5.5 billion charge due to regulation banning sales of its H20 AI accelerators to China. Another chipmaker, AMD, also falls 7%. Energy is the only gaining sector, up 1% as oil futures rise. Treasury yields fall. Jobless claims tomorrow are expected to increase slightly. Nasdaq falls 3%, to 16307; S&P 500 is down 2%, to 5276; and the DJIA sheds 700 points, or 2%, to 39669. U.S. markets will close on Good Friday. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1454 ET - Elevated inflation and consumer-packaged goods companies' focus on price over volume in the past few years is prompting consumers to pullback, Truist analysts say in a research note, citing General Mills' most recent earnings report that missed expectations and signaled a slowdown. Now, continued price increases tied to tariffs could prove to be the straw that breaks the camel's back, ultimately dragging the economy into a broader recession. Any downturn would likely result in weaker demand for paper and packaging companies, though the sector has proved resilient and capable of navigating tough times, the analysts write. They note that Packaging Corp. of America and Silgan are likely the best positioned to withstand a recession. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Australia's Insignia Financial extends exclusivity period for Bain, CC Capital bids
data.description: Australia's Insignia Financial said on Thursday that it has extended the exclusivity period by four weeks for both its bidders, Bain Capital and CC Capital Partners, to consider their proposals to acquire the wealth manager.

data.title: Nikkei Gains 0.2%, Led by Electronics, Heavy Industry StocksMarket Talk
data.description:
2021 ET - Japanese stocks are higher as some bargain hunting kicks in following their recent declines despite continuing uncertainty over U.S. tariffs. Electronics and heavy industry stocks are leading gains. TDK is up 3.0% and Lasertec is 1.5% higher while Kawasaki Heavy Industries is up 2.4% and IHI is 1.4% higher. USD/JPY is at 142.28, compared with 142.36 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any tariff-related news ahead of the earnings season set to start next week. The Nikkei Stock Average is up 0.2% at 33986.96. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

1942 ET - Japanese stocks may decline as uncertainty persists over U.S. tariffs and their economic implications. Nikkei futures are down 0.4% at 33820 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 141.69, down from 142.36 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any tariff-related news ahead of the earnings season set to start next week. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 1.0% to 33920.40 on Wednesday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1639 ET - In a speech and Q&A session in Chicago, Fed Chair Jerome Powell emphasized that keeping long-run inflation expectations in check has to be one of the central bank's overarching priorities. His comments suggest a fairly high bar for further monetary easing, analysts at LHMeyer write, indicating that Powell wants to "verify, not just trust, that high inflation won't persist." Policymakers want to be "certain that a one-time increase in the price level does not become an ongoing inflation problem," Powell said in his speech. In LHMeyer's view, his comments mean that "balancing the employment and inflation objectives in the current environment will likely involve tolerating more real-side weakness than otherwise." (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1443 ET - Treasury yields decline as Fed Chair Powell says at a conference in Chicago that tariffs could simultaneously raise prices and unemployment, making decisions on interest rates more difficult. He indicates the Fed would wait for more clarity before resuming interest-rate cutting. Odds of a hold in May are at 85%, up from 80% a week ago, CME data show. The 10-year yield is at 4.285% and the two-year at 3.794%, both erasing gains recorded over the past week. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1425 ET - The Bank of Canada is likely to keep its policy interest rate unchanged for an extended period, says Bank of Nova Scotia economist Derek Holt. He argues that after Canada's April 28 election, fiscal support for the economy is likely to intensify. The two main parties, Liberals and Conservatives, are promising tax relief and financial support for trade-exposed businesses and its workers. Fiscal policy will do some of the BOC's work in terms of providing economic support, Holt says. In a trade-conflict context, it's crucial the BOC be wary of cutting too deeply, he says, arguing this could trigger a new round of inflationary pressure akin to what transpired after the Covid-19 pandemic. (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1419 ET - The Bank of Canada decision to pause risks larger interest rate cuts later, Citi's Veronica Clark suggests. The economist says risks seem to be more clearly shifting to weaker inflation outcomes, and both of the scenarios presented by the central bank indicate Canadian growth slows and inflation is below 2% this year. With the current tariff backdrop likely to persist for at least three to six months and upcoming data set to weaken more clearly, Citi now expects a full percentage point of rate of cuts this year: a quarter point in June, half point in July and another quarter point in September. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1411 ET - Treasury yields decline as Fed Chair Powell speaks at a conference in Chicago. The monetary policymaker says tariffs could raise prices and unemployment at the same time, making decisions on interest rates harder to make. He indicates the Fed would wait for more clarity before resuming interest rate cutting. Odds of a hold in May are at 85%, up from 80% a week ago, CME data show. The 10-year yield is at 4.285% and the two-year at 3.794%, both erasing gains recorded over the past week. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1339 ET - The strength in March U.S. retail sales isn't only about consumers trying to get ahead of tariffs, Wells Fargo's Tim Quinlan and Shannon Grein write. Sales beat WSJ consensus in both the headline and the ex-auto figures. The report included upward revisions of previous data. Autos saw the biggest March gain, likely reflecting a rush to buy vehicles before any price increase due to tariffs. But consumers "also spent more at restaurants in March, and we're not aware of a way you can front-run tariffs with a nice night out," Quinlan and Grein say. "How consumers act in these next couple of months will be telling," they say. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1310 ET - The government of Canada's most populous province is moving to drop barriers to trade and labor mobility in the country with the introduction of a "Protect Ontario Through Free Trade Within Canada Act." Premier Doug Ford says the economy has long been held back by red tape and regulations, making Canada vulnerable to external threats, including from President Trump's tariffs. The move is part of a wave of support federally and provincially for easing trade within Canada to help counter the hit from Trump's trade policies. Ontario's act wold allow workers from other parts of the country to more easily work in the province, allow more regulated health professions to begin practicing in Ontario while waiting for registration, allow direct-to-consumer alcohol sales with reciprocating provinces, and remove all party-specific exceptions under the Canadian Free Trade Agreement. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1306 ET - Fitch Ratings says it lowered its forecasts for 2025 world growth by 0.4 percentage points, given the escalation in the global trade war. "World growth is projected to fall below 2% this year, which would be the weakest since 2009 excluding the pandemic," according to Fitch. Growth in the U.S. and in China has been cut by 0.5 pp, according to Fitch. Annual growth in the U.S. is expected at 1.2%, but will slow through 2025 to register just 0.4% year-over-year in 4Q25, Fitch says. In China, growth is expected to fall below 4% this year and in 2026. In the euro zone, growth expectations are stuck below 1%, Fitch adds. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1211 ET - As with the March decision, Bank of Canada senior officials debated whether to cut rates again, or hold their fire while awaiting more information. In the end, there was "clear consensus" on keeping the policy rate unchanged after a "really good deliberations" among top policymakers, says BOC's No. 2 official, Carolyn Rogers. She tells reporters there were differing opinions among the seven-member governing council on how big a hit there would be to the economy from US tariffs. Some officials, she says, were "more optimistic that the effects won't be really big." She adds some members suggested "you won't see that really big upward pressure on inflation." More details on the deliberations will emerge in two weeks when the BOC releases minutes of the governing council's discussions. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1207 ET - The Bank of Canada held its fire Wednesday and in doing so decided it would rather respond to the data as it happens and learn more about where trade policy is headed than try and be forward looking, CIBC Capital Markets' Avery Shenfeld says. The uncertainty was reflected in the central bank's monetary policy report, and Shenfeld notes both of the BoC's illustrative scenarios include a dent to growth and neither has inflation averaging far from 2% over the forecast horizon, a combination that might have justified another quarter point rate cut. As it stands, the economist wouldn't rule out half-point cut at the June meeting but given the BoC's caution he reckons it will deliver only a quarter point cut and a further quarter point cut in July. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

data.title: Meteoric Resources Target Price Raised 8.1% to A$0.40/Share by Barrenjoey>MEI.AU
JAPAN FISCAL 2024 TRADE DEFICIT Y5.22 TRILLION, 4TH STRAIGHT YEAR OF NEGATIVE BALANCE
JAPAN FISCAL 2024 IMPORTS 2ND LARGEST ON RECORD AT Y114.2 TRILLION, +4.7%, 1ST Y/Y RISE IN 2 YEARS
ANZ Research Adds Two More Rate Cuts to Forecast as Global Uncertainties Curtail New Zealand's Recovery Momentum
data.description:
ANZ Research sees more rates cuts than previously expected from the Reserve Bank of New Zealand as the road to recovery appears patchy amid persisting global uncertainties, according to a Wednesday report.

The firm penciled in additional rate cuts in August and October, taking the official cash rate (OCR) to a low of 2.5%, compared with its previous forecast of 3%.

It has "placeholder hikes" by end-2026 that will see the OCR back up to 3% by the fourth quarter.

ANZ said recent data suggests the country's economic recovery is "well underway" but is "looking a bit more stop-start than our current forecasts imply." It added that trade uncertainties pose a "darker and murkier outlook" for global growth.

"We therefore now think that the economy will require a bit more support from monetary policy to ensure that the recovery remains on track," ANZ wrote.

The firm accompanied its OCR revision with downgrades to its GDP and CPI inflation forecasts.

data.title: Lynas Rare Earths Target Price Raised 15% to A$9.20/Share by Euroz Hartleys>LYC.AU
